past_medical_history: Mild obstructive sleep apnea, uses CPAP intermittently
Anxiety disorder, well-controlled on current regimen
family_history: Father died suddenly at age 49 while jogging, cause listed as 'cardiac arrest' — no autopsy performed
Paternal uncle had a pacemaker placed at age 52 for unclear reasons
Mother alive at age 78, history of hypertension and osteoarthritis
No known family history of epilepsy or neurological disease

social_history.occupation: High school physical education teacher and basketball coach
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two teenage children in a two-story house
social_history.exercise: Very active — coaches basketball, plays recreational pickup games 2-3 times weekly, runs 3 miles most mornings

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent with normal rate, rhythm, and prosody. No dysarthria. Attention intact — recites months of the year backwards without error. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No aphasia. No confusion or post-ictal features at the time of evaluation, approximately 45 minutes after most recent event.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive 4mm to 3mm. Fundoscopy unremarkable, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact in all divisions bilaterally. Masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, no facial droop. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag reflex intact. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. No spasticity, rigidity, or fasciculations. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups bilaterally (deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger extensors, grip, interossei, hip flexors, knee extensors, knee flexors, ankle dorsiflexors, plantarflexors, toe extensors). No pronator drift. No drift on arm rolling test.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception intact in all four extremities. No sensory level. No dermatomal sensory loss.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin testing smooth bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base, stride, and arm swing. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration. No oral trauma. Nurse noted brief urinary incontinence during the second episode per EMS report — however, patient's wife clarifies that he had been drinking water heavily during the basketball game and may have had a full bladder. Small abrasion on left elbow from fall during the second event. No head trauma.

patient.chief_complaint: Two episodes of loss of consciousness with shaking, referred by outside hospital as 'new-onset seizures'
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Darnell Williams is a 55-year-old physically active man and high school basketball coach who presents to the emergency department after being transferred from an outside community hospital where he was evaluated for two episodes of loss of consciousness with witnessed 'shaking.' He was playing in a recreational basketball game approximately three hours ago when, during an intense sprint down the court, he suddenly collapsed forward without warning. Multiple witnesses, including fellow coaches, describe him falling to the ground and becoming rigid for 2-3 seconds followed by approximately 10-15 seconds of bilateral upper and lower extremity jerking movements. He was unresponsive during the episode. Upon regaining consciousness approximately 30 seconds after collapse, he appeared briefly dazed for about 30-60 seconds but then rapidly returned to baseline, asking 'what happened?' and wanting to get back into the game. He denied any chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath preceding the event. He reports no aura, no unusual taste or smell, and no preceding deja vu. Against advice, he rested on the bench for 15 minutes and then attempted to re-enter the game. During a fast break approximately 20 minutes later, he collapsed again with similar witnessed jerking lasting roughly 10 seconds. EMS was called and he was taken to the outside hospital where an ECG was obtained showing normal sinus rhythm at 76 bpm with no significant abnormalities. A CT head without contrast was unremarkable. He was loaded with levetiracetam 1000 mg IV and transferred here with a diagnosis of 'new-onset seizures, likely exercise-induced epilepsy.' His wife, who arrived at the outside hospital, reports that he has never had a seizure before but mentions that approximately six weeks ago he had a 'near-blackout' while running up the stairs at school carrying equipment, which he attributed to dehydration. She is visibly distressed and mentions that his father 'dropped dead' at age 49 while jogging. The patient's resting ECG obtained here in the ED again shows normal sinus rhythm with occasional premature ventricular complexes but no diagnostic arrhythmia. He is currently asymptomatic and appears well.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening and closing
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-amplitude beta activity, symmetric, likely enhanced by anxiety
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness captured with vertex waves and spindles; no epileptiform discharges in drowsiness or light sleep

artifacts.description: Intermittent muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity
Brief movement artifact when patient repositions, not epileptiform

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Symmetric bilateral slowing during hyperventilation, returning to baseline within 60 seconds of cessation. No epileptiform discharges provoked. Of note, the patient reported feeling lightheaded during hyperventilation but no clinical event occurred.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform abnormalities identified. No seizures captured during this 45-minute routine recording. A normal interictal EEG does not exclude epilepsy; however, the absence of any epileptiform features in the context of two closely spaced events makes epilepsy less likely as the sole explanation. Clinical correlation with cardiac evaluation is advised.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute study. Interictal EEG sensitivity for epilepsy is approximately 50% on a single recording. Exercise provocation was not performed during this recording. Prolonged or ambulatory monitoring could increase sensitivity if clinically indicated.
recommended_actions: If epilepsy remains a concern, consider prolonged ambulatory EEG or video-EEG monitoring
Given exertional triggers, strongly consider cardiac evaluation including prolonged cardiac monitoring and exercise stress testing
Correlation with family history of sudden cardiac death is critical

findings: Normal sinus rhythm at 76 bpm
Occasional premature ventricular complexes (3 PVCs during 10-second recording)
PVC morphology: left bundle branch block pattern with inferior axis, suggesting right ventricular outflow tract origin
PR interval normal
QRS duration normal
QTc 448 ms — borderline, at upper limit of normal
No ST-segment elevation or depression
No Brugada pattern
No epsilon waves appreciated
T-wave inversions in leads V1-V2 — may be normal variant or could suggest right ventricular pathology in the appropriate clinical context
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with occasional PVCs of RVOT morphology. QTc is borderline at 448 ms. Subtle T-wave inversions in V1-V2 are noted and may be a normal variant but cannot exclude right ventricular pathology such as arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC) in the appropriate clinical context. No definitive arrhythmia captured on this resting ECG. Given the history of exertional syncope with witnessed convulsive movements, the resting ECG does not capture the arrhythmia responsible for the events.
ecg.clinical_correlation: The normal resting ECG does not exclude a paroxysmal arrhythmia as the cause of exertional syncope. The PVCs, borderline QTc, and subtle anterior T-wave inversions may be clues to an underlying arrhythmic substrate. Given the strong family history of sudden cardiac death and exertional triggers, urgent further cardiac evaluation is strongly recommended.

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely stress-related or post-exertional; consistent with known prediabetes

Cardiac_markers.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, may reflect demand ischemia during arrhythmia or post-exertional; serial troponins recommended to assess trend
Within normal limits, argues against significant heart failure
Upper range of normal, may be elevated from vigorous exercise

Metabolic.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely post-exertional. A mildly elevated post-ictal lactate can also be seen after seizures, making this nonspecific.

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results do not reveal a metabolic or toxic cause for the patient's episodes. Electrolytes are normal, ruling out hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, or hypocalcemia as arrhythmic triggers. Troponin I is mildly elevated at 0.06 ng/mL, which may reflect demand ischemia during a transient arrhythmia or post-exertional elevation — serial troponins are recommended. Lactate is mildly elevated, likely post-exertional, though post-ictal lactic acidosis cannot be fully excluded. BNP is normal. Glucose is mildly elevated consistent with prediabetes and post-exercise state. Toxicology is negative.
abnormal_values_summary: Troponin I 0.06 ng/mL (mildly elevated, above upper limit of 0.04)

Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: At upper limit of normal but no definite LVH

Echocardiogram.value: Mildly dilated with mildly reduced systolic function (TAPSE 16 mm)
Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mild RV dilation with mildly reduced function — in the setting of T-wave inversions in V1-V2 and exertional syncope, raises concern for arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC)

Echocardiogram.value: Normal, trileaflet, no stenosis or regurgitation
Subtle RV free wall hypokinesis noted
Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Supports concern for ARVC; cardiac MRI recommended for further characterization

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram shows preserved left ventricular systolic function with an LVEF of 58%. Left ventricular dimensions and wall thickness are within normal limits. Notably, the right ventricle is mildly dilated with mildly reduced systolic function (TAPSE 16 mm) and subtle RV free wall hypokinesis. These findings, in the context of exertional syncope with convulsive features, T-wave inversions in V1-V2, PVCs of RVOT morphology, and a family history of sudden cardiac death, raise significant concern for arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC). Cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement is strongly recommended for further evaluation.
abnormal_values_summary: RV mildly dilated with reduced function (TAPSE 16 mm)

output.rhythm: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm with frequent premature ventricular complexes and one run of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
output.axis: Normal sinus axis; PVC axis rightward and superior
findings: Average heart rate 72 bpm (range 52-118 bpm)
Total PVCs: 2,847 in 24 hours (approximately 3.3% PVC burden)
PVC morphology: monomorphic, LBBB pattern with inferior axis (RVOT origin)
One run of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (NSVT): 8 beats at 185 bpm, occurring at 14:22 while patient was walking in the hallway
Three couplets of PVCs
No sustained ventricular tachycardia captured
No supraventricular tachycardia
No significant pauses (longest RR interval 1.4 seconds during sleep)
No AV block
Patient reported no symptoms during the NSVT episode
output.interpretation: 24-hour Holter monitoring reveals frequent PVCs (2,847/24h, 3.3% burden) with a monomorphic LBBB/inferior axis morphology consistent with RVOT origin. A single 8-beat run of NSVT at 185 bpm was captured during mild physical activity. The NSVT morphology matches the PVC morphology. No sustained VT was captured, though the patient was at rest or performing minimal activity during the monitoring period. In the context of exertional syncope, family history of sudden cardiac death, and echocardiographic findings of RV dysfunction, this PVC burden and NSVT are concerning for an arrhythmogenic substrate, particularly ARVC. Exercise stress testing and cardiac MRI are essential next steps.
output.clinical_correlation: The NSVT during mild exertion is significant. The patient's syncopal events occurred during vigorous exercise, and it is highly likely that a more sustained episode of ventricular tachycardia was provoked during intense physical activity, resulting in hemodynamic compromise and convulsive syncope. The absence of sustained VT on Holter is because the patient was not exercising vigorously during the recording. Exercise stress testing may reproduce the arrhythmia.

output.rhythm: Sinus rhythm progressing to polymorphic ventricular tachycardia requiring termination of test
findings: Resting: Normal sinus rhythm at 72 bpm with occasional PVCs
Stage 1 (3.0 min): Sinus tachycardia 108 bpm, PVC frequency increased (PVC bigeminy intermittently)
Stage 2 (6.0 min): Sinus tachycardia 132 bpm, couplets and triplets of PVCs emerged
Stage 3 (7.5 min — TEST TERMINATED): At heart rate 156 bpm, patient developed 12-beat run of monomorphic VT (LBBB morphology, 210 bpm) that degenerated into polymorphic VT lasting 18 seconds
Patient became pale, eyes rolled back, and had 3-4 seconds of tonic posturing followed by brief bilateral limb jerking
VT terminated spontaneously and patient rapidly regained consciousness within 15 seconds
Defibrillator pads applied but defibrillation not required
Post-event ECG showed sinus rhythm 88 bpm with frequent PVCs
Blood pressure during VT fell to 62/40 mmHg, recovered to 128/78 mmHg within 2 minutes of VT termination
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test provoked hemodynamically significant ventricular tachycardia with clinical syncope and convulsive features. The arrhythmia began as monomorphic VT at 210 bpm with LBBB morphology (RVOT origin) and briefly degenerated into polymorphic VT before self-terminating. The patient exhibited the same clinical features as his presenting events — transient loss of consciousness with tonic posturing and brief jerking movements (convulsive syncope). This confirms that the patient's 'seizures' are in fact convulsive syncope due to exercise-induced ventricular tachycardia. This is a very high-risk finding. Immediate cessation of all vigorous physical activity and urgent electrophysiology consultation are mandatory.
output.clinical_correlation: This test definitively reproduces the patient's clinical events. The exercise-induced VT explains every aspect of the presentation: exertional trigger, sudden collapse, convulsive movements (from cerebral hypoperfusion), brief duration, and rapid recovery. Combined with the echocardiographic RV abnormalities, PVCs of RVOT origin, anterior T-wave inversions, and family history of sudden cardiac death at age 49, this presentation is highly suspicious for arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC). Cardiac MRI and genetic testing are essential.

findings.location: RV free wall, basal to mid segments, extending to the subtricuspid region
findings.size: Involving approximately 40% of the RV free wall

signal_characteristics.T1: Hyperintense signal in RV free wall consistent with fatty infiltration
signal_characteristics.cine: Regional RV free wall dyskinesia with focal aneurysmal bulging at the subtricuspid region
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Extensive RV free wall late gadolinium enhancement consistent with fibrosis interspersed with fatty infiltration

findings.borders: Diffuse involvement of the RV free wall

findings.size: RVEDV 124 mL/m² (major criterion: >110 mL/m² in males)

volumetrics.RVEDV: 124 mL/m² (severely increased, major criterion for ARVC)

additional_observations: Regional RV wall motion abnormalities: dyskinesia of the RV free wall with focal aneurysmal change at subtricuspid region (major criterion)
LV is normal in size and function with no late gadolinium enhancement
output.impression: Cardiac MRI demonstrates findings meeting major Task Force Criteria for arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC): (1) regional RV dyskinesia with focal aneurysmal bulging, (2) RVEDV >110 mL/m², (3) RVEF <40%, and (4) extensive fibro-fatty replacement of the RV free wall with late gadolinium enhancement. Left ventricular size and function are preserved. These findings, combined with the clinical presentation of exercise-induced ventricular tachycardia causing convulsive syncope, the family history of sudden cardiac death, and the ECG findings (T-wave inversions V1-V2, PVCs of RVOT origin), establish a definitive diagnosis of ARVC. ICD implantation is indicated for secondary prevention of sudden cardiac death.
recommended_actions: Urgent electrophysiology consultation for ICD implantation (secondary prevention)
Genetic testing for desmosomal gene mutations (PKP2, DSP, DSG2, DSC2, JUP)
First-degree family screening with ECG, echocardiography, and cardiac MRI
Absolute restriction from competitive athletics and vigorous exercise
Consider antiarrhythmic therapy (sotalol or amiodarone) for VT suppression
Consider catheter ablation if recurrent VT despite medical therapy

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Meets multiple major revised Task Force Criteria by imaging, ECG, arrhythmia, and family history
No mediastinal lymphadenopathy, no LV involvement, no acute edema on T2, clinical picture more consistent with ARVC
Structural RV abnormalities on MRI exclude a benign idiopathic RVOT VT diagnosis

Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Deferred due to concern for catecholamine-triggered arrhythmias given clinical history

output.interpretation: Tilt table test is negative for vasovagal syncope or orthostatic hypotension. The patient maintained stable blood pressure and appropriate heart rate response throughout the 20-minute tilt protocol. Isoproterenol provocation was deferred due to concern for precipitating ventricular arrhythmia given the clinical context. This result makes vasovagal syncope unlikely as the diagnosis and supports an arrhythmic etiology for the patient's exertional syncope.

output.query: arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy exertional syncope convulsive movements misdiagnosed seizure

results.title: Convulsive syncope mimicking epilepsy in arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy
results.authors: Corrado D, Thiene G, et al.
results.journal: Journal of the American College of Cardiology
results.summary: Case series of 14 patients with ARVC initially misdiagnosed with epilepsy. Convulsive movements occurred in 78% of syncopal events due to cerebral hypoperfusion during sustained VT. Mean time to correct diagnosis was 2.3 years.

results.title: Distinguishing cardiac syncope from epileptic seizures: clinical and diagnostic clues
results.authors: Sheldon R, Rose S, et al.
results.summary: Prospective study showing that exertional trigger, duration of LOC <30 seconds, absence of post-ictal confusion >2 minutes, and absence of aura favor cardiac syncope over epilepsy. Family history of sudden death was present in 35% of cardiac syncope cases.

results.title: 2020 Revised Task Force Criteria for ARVC: diagnostic sensitivity and role of cardiac MRI
results.authors: Marcus FI, McKenna WJ, et al.
results.summary: Updated criteria emphasizing the role of cardiac MRI in detecting fibro-fatty replacement and RV wall motion abnormalities. RV volumes and RVEF by MRI have high diagnostic accuracy. Genetic testing positive in approximately 60% of definite ARVC cases.

output.summary: ARVC is an important cause of exertional syncope and sudden cardiac death, particularly in young to middle-aged patients and athletes. Convulsive syncope (brief tonic-clonic movements due to cerebral hypoperfusion during sustained VT) is commonly misdiagnosed as epilepsy, with reported diagnostic delays averaging 2-3 years. Key diagnostic clues include exertional triggers, family history of sudden cardiac death, PVCs of LBBB/RVOT morphology, T-wave inversions in V1-V3, and RV structural abnormalities on imaging. Cardiac MRI is the gold standard for detecting the fibro-fatty replacement characteristic of ARVC. The revised Task Force Criteria require a combination of structural, ECG, arrhythmic, genetic, and family history criteria for definitive diagnosis.

interactions: No significant pharmacokinetic interactions with amlodipine, losartan, sertraline, or aspirin
warnings: Levetiracetam is not indicated if the diagnosis is cardiac syncope rather than epilepsy
Unnecessary antiepileptic therapy may cause side effects (somnolence, behavioral changes) without benefit
Levetiracetam will not prevent arrhythmic syncope and may provide false reassurance, delaying appropriate cardiac evaluation
alternatives: If diagnosis is confirmed as cardiac syncope due to ARVC: discontinue levetiracetam and initiate antiarrhythmic therapy (sotalol, amiodarone) and ICD implantation
If epilepsy cannot be fully excluded: consider ambulatory EEG prior to committing to long-term AED therapy

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to exercise-induced ventricular tachycardia in the setting of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC)

differential.key_distinguishing: The witnessed shaking and urinary incontinence initially suggest seizure. However, the exertional trigger, very brief LOC (<30 seconds), rapid return to baseline without prolonged post-ictal confusion, absence of aura, and normal EEG argue against epilepsy. Convulsive syncope explains the motor phenomena.
No clear vasovagal trigger (pain, emotional stress, prolonged standing). Events occurred during vigorous exercise, which is atypical for vasovagal syncope. Negative tilt table test.
Can cause exertional syncope and sudden death. Echocardiogram did not show LV hypertrophy or LVOT obstruction. Cardiac MRI shows RV rather than LV pathology.

QTc is borderline (448 ms) but not clearly prolonged. Exercise-induced VT in LQTS is typically polymorphic (torsades de pointes), and the primary structural substrate here is RV fibro-fatty replacement consistent with ARVC.
CPVT causes exercise-induced bidirectional or polymorphic VT, typically in younger patients with structurally normal hearts. This patient has structural RV abnormalities on MRI, excluding CPVT.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately upon arrival and place on continuous telemetry
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with occasional PVCs and subtle T-wave inversions V1-V2; no diagnostic arrhythmia captured at rest

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, magnesium, lactate, TSH, and toxicology screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mildly elevated troponin (demand-related), mildly elevated lactate (post-exertional), no metabolic cause identified

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity given the referral diagnosis of seizures

Critically re-evaluate the seizure diagnosis: recognize that exertional trigger, brief LOC with rapid recovery, normal EEG, and family history of sudden cardiac death suggest cardiac syncope rather than epilepsy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical reasoning shifts from neurological to cardiac etiology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate cardiac structure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild RV dilation and dysfunction, raising concern for ARVC

optimal_actions.action: Order Holter monitor or continuous cardiac monitoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Frequent PVCs and non-sustained VT of RVOT morphology

optimal_actions.action: Order exercise stress test to provoke the exertion-related arrhythmia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Exercise-induced sustained VT reproducing the clinical event

optimal_actions.action: Order cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement to evaluate for ARVC
optimal_actions.expected_finding: RV fibro-fatty replacement, regional dyskinesia, RV dilation — meeting major Task Force Criteria for ARVC

optimal_actions.action: Discontinue levetiracetam (started inappropriately at outside hospital) and restrict patient from all vigorous physical activity
Urgent electrophysiology consultation for ICD implantation (secondary prevention) and consider antiarrhythmic therapy
Order genetic testing for desmosomal mutations and initiate first-degree family screening

critical_actions: Recognize that exertional syncope with convulsive features and family history of sudden cardiac death requires urgent cardiac evaluation, not just a seizure workup
Do not discharge with a diagnosis of epilepsy without cardiac evaluation
Restrict the patient from all competitive athletics and vigorous exercise immediately
Obtain cardiac imaging (echocardiogram and cardiac MRI) to evaluate for structural heart disease
Arrange electrophysiology consultation for ICD evaluation given high risk of sudden cardiac death
Screen first-degree family members given the heritable nature of ARVC
contraindicated_actions: Continuing long-term antiepileptic drug therapy without cardiac evaluation — misdiagnosis as epilepsy can be fatal
Allowing return to competitive sports or vigorous exercise before cardiac evaluation is complete
Discharging the patient home with a seizure diagnosis and outpatient neurology follow-up only
Administering isoproterenol or high-dose catecholamines without defibrillator backup (risk of VT/VF)
Prescribing QT-prolonging medications given borderline QTc and arrhythmic substrate
Performing a lumbar puncture (no indication — this is not a neurological condition)
key_reasoning_points: Exertional syncope is a red flag for cardiac arrhythmia — seizures triggered specifically by exercise are rare, whereas exercise-induced VT is a well-known presentation of ARVC
Convulsive syncope (brief tonic-clonic movements from cerebral hypoperfusion during VT) is commonly misdiagnosed as epileptic seizures, leading to dangerous diagnostic delays
The rapid return to full alertness within 30-60 seconds without prolonged post-ictal confusion is characteristic of cardiac syncope, not epilepsy
A normal resting ECG does not exclude a paroxysmal arrhythmia — the PVCs of RVOT morphology and subtle T-wave inversions in V1-V2 are important diagnostic clues that can be easily overlooked
The family history of sudden cardiac death at age 49 during exercise is a critical clue pointing to an inherited arrhythmic condition such as ARVC
The mildly elevated troponin likely reflects demand ischemia during the VT episode, not a primary coronary event
Urinary incontinence, while more commonly associated with seizures, can also occur during cardiac syncope and should not be used as a sole discriminator

The outside hospital's decision to load levetiracetam before completing cardiac evaluation represents a common diagnostic pitfall — anchoring on the seizure diagnosis based on the witnessed convulsive movements

metadata.author: NeuroBench generation pipeline
clinical_pearls: ARVC is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in athletes under age 35 in some European registries
Convulsive syncope occurs in up to 12% of all syncopal events and is a major cause of misdiagnosis as epilepsy

The Revised Task Force Criteria for ARVC use a combination of major and minor criteria across six categories: structural, tissue characterization, repolarization, depolarization, arrhythmia, and family history/genetics
Competitive athletic restriction is a Class I recommendation in ARVC because exercise accelerates disease progression and increases arrhythmic risk
First-degree family members of ARVC patients should undergo screening with ECG, echocardiography, and cardiac MRI even if asymptomatic
